subject: ap-government
title: SCOTUS Comparison — Timbs v. Indiana (2019)
stimulus:
In November of 2013, Tyson Timbs was arrested after selling illegal drugs to an undercover police officer. Timbs pleaded guilty to the charges and was sentenced to a year of house arrest, five years probation, and a $1,200 fine.

In addition, the state sought to seize Timbs's Land Rover, which Timbs used to transport the illegal drugs. The Land Rover was previously purchased for $42,000. Timbs argued that the vehicle seizure constituted an excessive fine, which was a violation of the Eighth Amendment. The maximum fine for Timbs's crime was $10,000, which was considerably less than the price Timbs paid for the Land Rover.

In the resulting case, Timbs v. Indiana (2019), the Supreme Court unanimously ruled in favor of Timbs. The opinion of the Court, authored by Justice Ruth Bader Ginsburg, stated that the excessive fines clause is a provision that applies to the states. The Court found that the protection against excessive fines is "fundamental to our scheme of ordered liberty" and "deeply rooted in this Nation's history and tradition."

parts.prompt: Identify the legal doctrine that is common in both Timbs v. Indiana (2019) and McDonald v. Chicago (2010).
parts.scoring_notes: Only selective incorporation (or incorporation doctrine) earns the point. Due process alone does not earn the point.

scoring_points.description: Identify the legal doctrine that is common in both Timbs v. Indiana (2019) and McDonald v. Chicago (2010).

required_elements: Identifies selective incorporation (or the incorporation doctrine) as the legal doctrine common to both cases
alternatives.correct_example: Selective incorporation is the legal doctrine common to both Timbs v. Indiana and McDonald v. Chicago.

wrong_examples: Due process — while both cases use the Due Process Clause as the vehicle for incorporation, the legal doctrine in question is selective incorporation itself.
Federalism — while incorporation implicates federalism, the specific legal doctrine is selective incorporation.
common_traps: Accept 'selective incorporation,' 'incorporation doctrine,' or 'incorporating the Bill of Rights to the states'
Do not accept just 'due process' without reference to incorporating the Bill of Rights

parts.prompt: Explain how the holding in McDonald was similar to the holding in Timbs.
parts.scoring_notes: First point requires accurate information about McDonald v. Chicago (the required case). Second point requires connecting both cases through the doctrine of selective incorporation — both applied Bill of Rights provisions to the states through the Fourteenth Amendment.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the holding in McDonald was similar to the holding in Timbs. (1 point — describe relevant fact or holding about the required Supreme Court case McDonald v. Chicago)

required_elements: Accurately describes the holding or facts from McDonald v. Chicago, the required case
alternatives.correct_example: In McDonald v. Chicago, the Supreme Court held that the Second Amendment right to keep and bear arms for self-defense applies to state and local governments through the Fourteenth Amendment.

required_elements: Accurately describes the holding or facts from McDonald v. Chicago, the required case
alternatives.correct_example: McDonald challenged a Chicago handgun ban, and the Court incorporated the Second Amendment against the states, ruling that Chicago's ban violated the right to keep and bear arms.

wrong_examples: Describes only Timbs without mentioning McDonald — earns 0 points.
Confuses McDonald with District of Columbia v. Heller, which was about federal law, not incorporation.
common_traps: Only McDonald information is needed for the first point — a full comparison is not yet required
Accept descriptions of the holding or the facts

scoring_points.description: Explain how the holding in McDonald was similar to the holding in Timbs. (2 points — correctly explains how BOTH cases led to similar holdings through selective incorporation)

required_elements: Explains that both cases involved the Court applying a Bill of Rights provision to the states through the Fourteenth Amendment
alternatives.correct_example: In McDonald, the Court incorporated the Second Amendment right to keep and bear arms against the states, holding that Chicago's handgun ban violated that right. In Timbs, the Court similarly incorporated the Eighth Amendment's excessive fines clause against the states, holding that Indiana's vehicle seizure was subject to that constitutional limit. Both cases are similar because the Court used selective incorporation to extend Bill of Rights protections to state government actions.

wrong_examples: Describes only one case in detail — earns 1 point (b1) only.
States both cases are about 'rights' without explaining the selective incorporation connection.
common_traps: Must address BOTH cases to earn the second point
The key similarity is that both cases used selective incorporation to apply a specific Bill of Rights provision to the states

parts.prompt: Explain how the decision in Timbs affects the reserve powers of the states.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must explain the specific limitation on state power created by the Timbs decision — states' sentencing and asset forfeiture powers are now constrained by the Eighth Amendment's excessive fines clause.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the decision in Timbs affects the reserve powers of the states.

required_elements: Explains that the Timbs decision limits states' power to impose fines or seize property by subjecting those powers to the Eighth Amendment's excessive fines clause
alternatives.correct_example: By incorporating the excessive fines clause against the states, Timbs limits the reserve powers of states in the area of criminal sentencing and asset forfeiture. States can no longer impose fines or seize property that is grossly disproportionate to the offense — they must comply with the Eighth Amendment's excessive fines standard.

required_elements: Explains that Timbs restricts state law enforcement powers by applying a federal constitutional limit
alternatives.correct_example: The Timbs decision constrains a state's reserve power to determine criminal punishments. Before the ruling, states had more discretion in their asset forfeiture laws. After Timbs, those laws must comply with the Eighth Amendment's protection against excessive fines, reducing states' ability to seize property of disproportionate value as a punishment.

wrong_examples: Simply stating that 'the states have less power now' without explaining how or in what area.
Describing the facts of the Timbs case without connecting to the concept of reserve powers.
common_traps: Reserve powers are the powers retained by states under the Tenth Amendment — the response must explain how Timbs limits one of those powers
The specific limitation is on criminal punishment/sentencing/forfeiture powers — not general state authority
